title: When Fury Meets the Dark Night of the Soul: The Body’s Secret Reckoning
dek: You wake at 3 AM, ribs tightening around a breath that feels both too shallow and too sharp. Your psoas clenches like a coiled wire, a subterranean ache that refuses to soften. This is not just sorrow or burnout  -  this is the body’s unyielding cry during the dark night of the soul, where anger isn’t failure but the raw, aching messenger of transformation.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Midnight Grip of the Psoas


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.978Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

It’s the hour when the world slips into silence, but your body refuses. You lie in bed, the clock’s red digits glaring back at you like a judgment. Your diaphragm pulls uneven breaths, shallow and brittle, like dry leaves scraping across gravel. Beneath the surface, your psoas muscle - a deep core stabilizer running from the lumbar spine to the femur - contracts with the persistence of a clenched fist. It’s a muscle that doesn’t lie: it remembers the primal fight or flight, the way your nervous system is still wired for survival long after the perceived threat has passed.

The psoas holds your story of tension, the exhaustion that settles like sediment in the hips and lower back. When the dark night of the soul comes, this muscle tightens as if to shield you from the unknown abyss you stare into with wide, unblinking eyes. Your legs feel leaden, heavy with inertia, even though your mind races through every possible unraveling. This ache is not weakness; it is the body’s way of grounding fury that has nowhere to go, the physical form of a heart breaking open in slow motion.

## The Masseter’s Silent Complaint

As your jaw clenches, the masseter muscle - thick and powerful, the prime mover of your chewing apparatus - becomes a battleground. You might not notice it during the day, but at night, when your mind is unspooling, the tension gathers there like a storm cloud. The tightness presses against your molars, a dull throb that can erupt into a sharp, relentless pain. This clenched masseter is your body's unspoken language of anger and resistance, the stubbornness of refusing to soften or let go.

Karla McLaren, a teacher of emotional intelligence, speaks of anger as a messenger, not a villain. When your jaw tightens in the dark, it’s not you failing to relax - it’s your body holding a fierce conversation with itself. Your anger is not a moral failing but a somatic truth that your nervous system cannot dismiss. This tension is the body’s way of saying, "I am here. I am alert. I am not invisible." The masseter remembers the moments when you bit back words, swallowed your fury, and held your silence like a shield. Now, it protests in the only way it knows.

## The 3 AM Wake-Up Call

There is a sacred cruelty in waking at 3 AM, that liminal hour when the veil between conscious thought and raw sensation thins. You are ripped from sleep not by dreams but by the visceral knowledge that something inside you demands attention. The skin on your arms feels too tight, the trapezius and levator scapulae muscles knotting around your neck and shoulders, as if bracing against an invisible weight. Your breath is shallow, the diaphragm spasming in quick, uneven bursts.

This is the body’s dark night of the soul: a reckoning that refuses to be silenced or sanitized. Pema Chödrön’s teachings remind us that discomfort is the door to awakening, but when you are in the middle of it, the door feels like a trap. The exhaustion is bone-deep, the kind that seeps into the marrow and whispers that you can’t keep going as you have. Your body wakes you - sometimes violently - because it knows the fury you were taught to hide is the very force that will carry you through. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows what the mind cannot yet understand.

## The Body’s Reckoning: When Anger Meets the Dark Night

You don’t just “feel” the dark night of the soul; you live it in the raw geography of your body. The night arrives like a thick fog settling in the psoas, that deep core muscle holding your spine and hips together. Your diaphragm contracts unevenly as if caught in a silent scream, the breath shallow and tethered close to the collarbones. The trapezius tightens, knitting a noose of tension from the nape of your neck down to your shoulder blades. This is not metaphor - this is biology. The body remembers what the mind cannot yet name.

Anger, in this context, is not the enemy. It is the body’s urgent messenger, the primal knot of heat and pressure screaming beneath the surface. Robert Augustus Masters, a somatic psychotherapist whose work I trust deeply, teaches that anger during the dark night is the body’s way of breaking through frozen patterns of shame, grief, and numbing dissociation. Masters insists that the dark night is not merely a psychological crisis but a somatic one - anger serves as the fire to melt the ice crusting over your soul’s deepest wounds. He urges us not to suppress this fury but to allow it to move through the anatomy of our being, through the tight bands of fascia and the fibrous sheath of the masseter muscle that clenches during silent rage.

At night, when the world quiets and the familiar distractions vanish, you may find the levator scapulae muscles along your neck stiffening with the weight of unresolved fury. This is the body’s unspoken rebellion against the loneliness of the dark night. You might reach for herbal allies like Motherwort, a nervine known for its ability to soothe the heart’s overworked rhythm and ease the constriction of the chest. Its bittersweet tincture tastes like the earth itself, grounding and softening the harsh edges of internal turmoil. A dose of [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) before bed can help ease the diaphragm’s spasms and invite a more even breath, a small reprieve when the night feels endless.

## The Fierce Compassion of Pema Chödrön’s Anger

Pema Chödrön, the fierce and tender Buddhist teacher, offers a radical reframing of anger during the dark night. She doesn’t ask you to shun your fury or to pretend you’re serene. Instead, she invites you to meet it with what she calls “loving-kindness with teeth.” The dark night is a time when the usual safeguards - denial, distraction, the polite social mask - fall away, and your anger shines with raw clarity. It is in this exposed state that you can begin to recognize anger’s true nature: not as a destructive force but as a boundary marker, a call to honor your own limits and truths.

Chödrön’s teachings emphasize the somatic experience of anger - the heat rising to the face, the tightness in the chest, the clenching of the jaw (masseter muscle again, that old betrayer). She encourages you to lean into these sensations, to observe the physical nature of your fury without judgment. “We’re often afraid of anger because it’s so raw and unpredictable,” she writes, but “when we sit with it, the body softens, and the heart opens.” This is a kind of fierce compassion, one that does not flinch from the shadow but embraces it fully.

Here, herbs like Ashwagandha become invaluable. Ashwagandha, an adaptogen revered in Traditional Chinese Medicine and Ayurveda, supports the adrenal glands and calms the nervous system without dulling the vital edge of your anger. Its earthy, robust flavor mirrors its grounding effect on the nervous system, helping you stay present in the storm rather than fleeing into dissociation or numbing habits. I often recommend a gentle Ashwagandha tonic like [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) during the day for women navigating the dark night. It does not tame your fire but steadies the hand that holds the flame.

## Navigating the Night with Skullcap and Somatic Awareness

The dark night does not relent easily. It can stretch across weeks or months, each day a long exhale of grief and fury twisting in the levator scapulae and the sternocleidomastoid muscles of the neck. You might feel the sharp catch of old anger lodged in the scapular region, a tether pulling you backward into past betrayals or losses. Resmaa Menakem’s work on racialized trauma reminds us that the body carries not only personal pain but intergenerational and cultural suffering as well. Anger in the dark night can be a portal to this collective anguish, a somatic echo that vibrates through your fascia like a discordant chord.

Skullcap, a gentle yet potent nervine, becomes a trusted companion here. It quiets the restless mind and soothes the nervous system without sedating, allowing you to stay awake to your experience rather than slipping into exhaustion or avoidance. When the trapezius and masseter muscles clench in the early hours of the morning, a dropperful of [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) tincture can ease the tension and invite a more spacious breath.

As Karla McLaren, an empathy researcher and author, teaches, anger is a “guide” and “guardian” in our emotional landscape. During the dark night, she insists, anger demands your full attention - it wants to be understood, not silenced. McLaren’s approach is deeply somatic: she encourages tracking the physical sensations of anger, mapping where it lives in your body, what textures it creates beneath your skin, and how it shifts with breath and movement. This practice transforms anger from a shadow into a language, a way to navigate the dark night’s labyrinth with curiosity and courage.

So when your jaw tightens at 3 a.m., when your psoas knots into a steel coil at dawn, when the levator scapulae scream with old grief, listen closely. The dark night is speaking, and your anger is its most honest dialect. You do not have to fix or flee it - only bear witness, with the help of the body’s wisdom and the earth’s medicines, until the dawn comes.

## When Anger Meets the Dark Night of the Soul

You know that crushing moment when your anger doesn’t roar out in a fit of rage, but instead sinks into a strange, cold silence within you? That’s the dark night of the soul  -  not the poetic nicety of a spiritual crisis, but the deep, visceral unraveling of your inner landscape. It’s the moment your body clamps down, the masseter muscles tight and unforgiving, the trapezius muscles knotting at the base of your neck like a noose, and your diaphragm locked in a frozen cage of breathlessness. This isn’t a failure of spirit or character; it’s the body’s way of telling you that something crucial is breaking open.

In this darkness, anger doesn’t vanish  -  it transforms, withdrawing from the surface to burrow deep into your fascia, embedding itself in the tight bands that run beneath your skin and between your ribs. The body remembers, even when the mind wants to forget. Bessel van der Kolk, in his work on trauma, reminds us that “the body keeps the score.” When you feel that quiet, heavy crush of the dark night, it’s your nervous system desperately trying to process the unprocessable. The levator scapulae muscles, those small but stubborn fibers that lift your shoulders, seize up, locking you into a state of hypervigilance or shutdown. Your jaw clenches; you taste the metallic tang of adrenaline in your mouth. This is not pathology  -  this is biology screaming for release.

What’s often misunderstood is that this dark night is not the absence of anger; it is anger in its rawest, most unadorned form. It is the body’s way of saying, “I am breaking so that I might be whole.” This is not a failure to be fixed, but a process to be witnessed. Resmaa Menakem’s work on racialized trauma teaches us that trauma and anger are held in the body, especially in the body’s “blue zone”  -  the parts of us where we feel shame, rage, and grief. He points to the importance of somatic awareness, of feeling the body’s tremors and twitches, the faint buzz of energy that signals the beginning of healing. Your dark night is a somatic experience, a subterranean storm that, though it feels isolating, connects you deeply with the life force pulsing beneath your skin.

## Anger as a Compass Through the Abyss

When you are in the depths of the dark night, anger can serve as a compass, a visceral guide pointing you toward the unseen wounds that demand your attention. Gabor Maté’s teachings about addiction and trauma illuminate this path: he speaks of anger not as a destructive force but as a signal, a message from the parts of you that have been silenced or ignored. The body’s tension, the fluttering in your solar plexus, the sharp edge behind your eyes late at night  -  these are the coordinates of your internal map, marking where your boundaries were crossed or where your needs were unmet.

The dark night is a place without easy answers, where your anger’s usual outlets may feel closed, and where your mind’s habitual narratives fall away. But the body remembers. Your psoas muscles, those deep core muscles that hold your primal survival instincts, contract in response to fear, grief, and rage. When you surrender to this contraction, when you allow yourself to feel the full weight of your body’s response, you tap into a deep wisdom that no amount of rationalizing can reach. This is the place where anger becomes medicine  -  not by being pushed down or exploded outward, but by being fully embodied, honored for its truth.

The dark night invites you to listen with more than your ears. It asks you to rest your hands on your chest and notice the subtle shifts in your ribcage as you breathe, to feel the slow, heavy pulse of your carotid artery in the quiet of 3 a.m. It asks you to witness the tremors in your forearms, the way your fingers curl when you’re overwhelmed, and to meet these signals without judgment. This is the practice of radical witnessing, a somatic presence that Pema Chödrön advocates  -  the willingness to be with discomfort, to hold your pain as if it were a fragile flame.

## Embodied Practice: Meeting the Dark Night with Your Body

Tonight, as the clock edges toward 2:37 a.m., lie down in a quiet room where the air is cool and carries the faint scent of chamomile tea or dried lavender. Begin by placing one hand over your heart and the other on your lower abdomen, just above your navel. Close your eyes gently and take a slow, deliberate breath through your nose, feeling your diaphragm drop and your belly expand beneath your hand. Exhale softly through your mouth, noticing the subtle release in your chest.

Now, bring your attention to your jaw. Notice if the masseter muscles feel clenched or tight. Without forcing anything, allow your jaw to part just a little, as if you’re about to sigh or speak a long-held truth. Feel the difference in tension. Let your tongue rest lightly against the roof of your mouth, and feel the slight shift in the floor of your mouth.

Shift your awareness to your shoulders and neck. Feel the trapezius and levator scapulae muscles, and imagine a warm herbal compress  -  perhaps infused with the calming properties of skullcap or passionflower  -  melting the knots and softening the tension. If you have a tincture like [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) nearby, you might take a small dose to support your nervous system’s unwinding.

As you breathe, invite the sensation of your anger to surface without resistance. It may feel like heat gathering behind your eyes or a heavy weight in your chest. Let it be as it is. If tears come, let them fall. If a shiver runs down your spine, welcome it as your body’s way of moving energy.

Stay with this sensation for as long as you can, breathing into the spaces where your body feels tight or numb. When you are ready, slowly open your eyes, wiggle your fingers and toes, and return to the room. You have met your anger in the dark night, and you are still here.

## The Dawn of Somatic Integration

The dark night of the soul is not a permanent state, though it often feels endless when you are caught in its grip. It is a necessary passage, a somatic crucible where the raw material of your anger is forged into something enduring and true. As the night begins to lift, you may notice a subtle shift in your body’s landscape. The masseter muscles, once locked in a silent scream, begin to soften. The psoas, tight with the memory of flight, slowly unfurls. The breath, once shallow and erratic, deepens, filling the lower lobes of your lungs with a steady, rhythmic cadence.

This is the dawn of somatic integration, the moment when the body begins to weave the fragmented pieces of your experience back into a cohesive whole. It is not a return to who you were before the dark night, but an evolution into someone who has faced the abyss and returned with a deeper understanding of their own resilience. The anger that once felt like a destructive force has become a trusted ally, a somatic compass that guides you toward your own truth.

In this new light, you can begin to see the dark night not as a punishment, but as a profound act of self-love. It is the body’s way of demanding that you honor your own limits, that you listen to the quiet voice beneath the noise of the world. And as you step forward into the day, you carry with you the wisdom of the night, the fierce compassion of a heart that has broken open and learned to heal itself.

## FAQ: Navigating Anger and the Dark Night

**Q: Why does my body feel so exhausted during the dark night of the soul?**
**A:** The exhaustion you feel is not just physical; it is a profound somatic response to the intense emotional and psychological work your body is doing. During the dark night, your nervous system is often in a state of hyperarousal or shutdown, which requires an immense amount of energy to maintain. The deep core muscles, like the psoas, are constantly engaged, bracing against the perceived threat of the unknown. This constant state of tension drains your reserves, leaving you feeling bone-tired. It is essential to honor this exhaustion by resting, not just sleeping, but engaging in restorative practices that allow your nervous system to down-regulate.

**Q: Is it normal to feel physically ill or in pain when I am angry?**
**A:** Yes, it is entirely normal. Anger is a powerful physiological response that triggers the release of stress hormones like adrenaline and cortisol. These hormones prepare the body for fight or flight, causing muscles to tense, heart rate to increase, and digestion to slow down. When anger is suppressed or unexpressed, this physiological arousal has nowhere to go, leading to chronic tension, headaches, digestive issues, and other physical symptoms. The body is literally holding onto the anger, and the pain is its way of signaling that something needs to be released.

**Q: How can I safely express my anger without causing harm to myself or others?**
**A:** Safe expression of anger begins with somatic awareness. Before you act on your anger, take a moment to locate it in your body. Notice where the tension is held - is it in your jaw, your chest, your stomach? Breathe into those areas and allow the physical sensation to exist without judgment. Once you have grounded yourself in your body, you can choose how to express the anger. This might involve physical movement, like shaking, dancing, or going for a brisk walk. It could involve vocalizing, such as sighing deeply, humming, or even screaming into a pillow. The goal is to move the energy through your body, rather than directing it outward in a destructive way.

**Q: Can herbs really help with the physical symptoms of anger and the dark night?**
**A:** Yes, herbs can be powerful allies in supporting the nervous system during times of intense emotional distress. Nervines like Skullcap, Motherwort, and Passionflower can help soothe the nervous system, ease muscle tension, and promote a sense of calm without sedating you. Adaptogens like Ashwagandha can support the adrenal glands and help the body adapt to stress over time. However, it is important to remember that herbs are not a cure-all; they are meant to be used in conjunction with somatic practices, emotional processing, and, when necessary, professional support. Always consult with a qualified herbalist or healthcare provider before starting any new herbal regimen.
